3A_Bibliographic.citation: Weigard, A., McCurry, K. L., Shapiro, Z., Martz, M. E., Angstadt, M., Heitzeg, M. M., Dinov, I. D., & Sripada, C. (2023). Generalizable prediction of childhood ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive testing and youth characteristics. Translational Psychiatry, 13, 225.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Screen time is a predictor variable in a machine learning model predicting ADHD symptoms, not the primary exposure-outcome of interest.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Data were from the curated data release version 4.0 of the ABCD Study, a large-scale consortium that recruited 11,878 children, ages 9-10, across 21 study sites. For the 'full data' sample with missing ADHD symptom data imputed (n = 8,972), average r values across left-out sites indicated that the models explained roughly 15-16% of the variance in symptoms.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Primary analyses use n=8,972 (full data with imputation); complete data subsample n=4,855.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Weekday and weekend screen time as two of 54 predictor features in a machine learning model
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time (weekday and weekend separately)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (hours); entered as one of 54 predictive features
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Children's estimates of their time spent engaged in screen media on typical weekdays and weekend days were obtained from the ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire.
3C_Exposure.notes: Screen time is NOT the primary exposure; it is one of 54 features in a predictive model. This paper is primarily about ADHD prediction, not screen time effects.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: ADHD symptoms (cross-rater factor score from CBCL and BPM Attention Problems)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Cross-rater bifactor structural equation model combining CBCL Attention Problems (parent) and BPM Attention/Hyperactivity Problems (teacher) items
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: We fit a bifactor structural equation model to parent-report items from the Attention Problems scale and ADHD DSM-oriented scale of the CBCL and teacher-report items from the Attention/Hyperactivity Problems scale of the BPM using the R package lavaan. This method allows common variance in ADHD symptoms indicated across raters to be measured via a general factor.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Machine learning: principal components regression (PCR) and LASSO with leave-one-site-out cross-validation
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: N/A - 54 features entered simultaneously including demographics, geocoded variables, neurocognitive tests, child self-report measures, biometrics
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We used two modeling strategies: principal components regression (PCR) and LASSO. We used leave-one-site-out cross-validation to train all models and test their generalizability. For each left-out ABCD site, predictive models were trained in data from all other sites and used to generate predicted values for the ADHD symptoms score in both the training data and the held-out test site.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: This is a prediction/machine learning paper, not a traditional regression analysis.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Full predictor set (54 features including screen time) - PCR model
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Correlation between predicted and actual ADHD symptom scores in left-out test sites
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: For the 'full data' sample with missing ADHD symptom data imputed (n = 8,972), average r values across left-out sites indicated that the models explained roughly 15-16% of the variance in symptoms. (PCR r = 0.40, CI = 0.38-0.42; LASSO r = 0.39, CI = 0.38-0.41)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Sparse LASSO model (13 features including screen time, sex, impulsivity, parental monitoring, neurocognitive)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: The final sparse LASSO model that included only these 13 features displayed a performance that was practically identical to the more complex PCR and LASSO models (r = 0.39, CI = 0.37-0.41).

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: This is a prediction/ML paper. The primary metric is prediction accuracy (r = 0.39-0.40, explaining 15-16% variance), which is evaluated by its practical utility for prediction rather than as an effect size. Screen time is one feature among many.

3H_Recommendations.notes: Paper notes results cannot be directly translated to clinical practice and that prediction models do not necessarily reflect causal relationships.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.population_override_verbatim: moderate improvements in prediction for individuals can be expected to cumulate (i.e., moderate improvements in prediction for individuals can translate to large overall impacts at the population level)
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: As some of these important predictors, including both screen media use and parental monitoring, may be malleable or responsive to behavioral interventions, they represent potential targets for treatment.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames findings as demonstrating that machine learning can effectively predict ADHD symptoms using diverse features. Screen time is discussed as one of several important predictors, alongside neurocognition, impulsivity, and parental monitoring. The paper notes that screen time is uniquely predictive and is a potentially malleable target. The paper appropriately notes that prediction models do not establish causal relationships and calls for caution in this area.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: This paper uses screen time as one predictor among many in a ML framework rather than as a primary exposure. The specific contribution of screen time to prediction is discussed qualitatively via feature weights but not quantified with a specific effect size for the screen-time-to-ADHD association.
